items.title: The Evolution of ClickFix: From Cleartext to Server Side Polymorphism
items.description: The ClickFix campaign has evolved from basic disk-based infections to sophisticated, obfuscated attacks using fake CAPTCHA pages that trick victims into executing malicious PowerShell commands. Initial variants used cleartext commands downloading batch scripts to deploy DeerStealer InfoStealer. The campaign advanced to fileless execution using XOR encryption or Base64 compression, operating entire
items.ai_summary: The ClickFix campaign has evolved to utilize sophisticated, obfuscated attacks, leveraging fake CAPTCHA pages to trick victims into executing malicious PowerShell commands, specifically utilizing T1059.001. The initial variants of this campaign used cleartext commands to download batch scripts for deploying the DeerStealer InfoStealer, while later variants adopted fileless execution techniques, incorporating XOR encryption or Base64 compression. To mitigate this threat, defenders should implement PowerShell Constrained Language mode and restrict the use of PowerShell for non-administrative tasks, ensuring that all PowerShell scripts are signed and validated. Additionally, monitoring for unusual patterns of PowerShell execution, particularly those invoking obfuscated scripts, is crucial. Real-world impact includes the potential for widespread data breaches and unauthorized access, with specific examples including the compromise of sensitive user information through the DeerStealer InfoStealer.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1059.001| B[Execution]:::tactic
    B -->|T1027| C[Defense Evasion]:::tactic
    C -->|T1059.004| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1041| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: The Gentleman Ransomware | Defense Evasion TTPs Uncovered
items.description: In April and May 2026, investigations revealed two incidents involving The Gentlemen ransomware-as-a-service operation, which has claimed over 400 victims across 70 countries since mid-2025. Both incidents demonstrated common tactics including Scheduled Tasks, PowerShell commands, and defense evasion techniques such as clearing Security, System, and Application Event Logs, disabling Microsoft Defe
items.ai_summary: The Gentleman ransomware-as-a-service operation has been linked to over 400 victims across 70 countries, utilizing tactics such as Scheduled Tasks and PowerShell commands to evade defenses. Specifically, the attackers employ T1053 to schedule malicious tasks, T1059.001 to execute PowerShell commands, and T1562 to impair defenses by clearing Security, System, and Application Event Logs. To remediate, defenders should immediately update their PowerShell versions to the latest, ensuring they have the latest security patches, and configure their systems to prevent the execution of unsigned PowerShell scripts. Additionally, implementing a robust logging and monitoring system can help detect such activities early on. It is also crucial to regularly back up critical data and ensure that backups are stored securely, offline, and are not accessible via the network to prevent data encryption by the attackers.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1588| B[Obtain Capabilities]:::tactic
    B -->|T1053| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1059.001| D[Defense Evasion]:::tactic
    D -->|T1562| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: One Man, One AI, One Fake Persona: Inside the 5-Year Influence and Fraud 'Patriot Bait' Campaign
items.description: A solo Russian-speaking threat actor tracked as 'bandcampro' operated a five-year MAGA-themed Telegram channel with approximately 17,000 subscribers, initially forwarding cryptocurrency scam content before pivoting to AI-automated operations in September 2025. The actor utilized jailbroken Google Gemini to generate QAnon-styled posts, deploy infrastructure, manage stolen API keys, and run credenti
items.ai_summary: The 'Patriot Bait' campaign, operated by a solo Russian-speaking threat actor known as 'bandcampro', leveraged a MAGA-themed Telegram channel with approximately 17,000 subscribers to spread cryptocurrency scam content and later AI-automated operations. The actor utilized jailbroken Google Gemini to generate QAnon-styled posts, deploy infrastructure, manage stolen API keys, and run credential harvesting operations. This campaign highlights the real-world impact of social engineering and influence operations, where threat actors can manipulate public opinion and exploit individuals for financial gain. To remediate, defenders should monitor for suspicious Telegram channel activity, implement robust API key management, and educate users on the dangers of cryptocurrency scams and AI-generated content. Additionally, defenders should consider implementing AI-powered detection tools to identify and flag potential influence operations.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1204| D[Credential Access]:::tactic
    D -->|T1110| E[Command and Control]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Middle East Malicious Infrastructure Report: 1,350+ C2 Servers Mapped Across 98 Providers
items.description: Between February and May 2026, over 1,350 active command-and-control servers were identified across 98 infrastructure providers spanning 14 Middle Eastern countries. Saudi Arabia's STC hosted 981 C2 servers, representing 72.4% of all regional malicious infrastructure, the largest concentration globally. C2 infrastructure dominated at 96.8% of detected activity, with IoT-focused botnets like Hajime
items.ai_summary: The Middle East Malicious Infrastructure Report reveals a significant presence of command-and-control (C2) servers across 98 infrastructure providers in 14 countries, with Saudi Arabia's STC hosting the largest concentration of 981 C2 servers, accounting for 72.4% of regional malicious infrastructure. The threat actors leveraged T1583 (Acquire Infrastructure) to obtain infrastructure and T1071 (Application Layer Protocol) to communicate with compromised devices, primarily targeting IoT devices with botnets like Hajime. If exploited, these C2 servers can lead to unauthorized access, data breaches, and disruption of critical infrastructure. To remediate, defenders should implement robust network monitoring, utilize threat intelligence feeds to identify known C2 servers, and configure firewalls to block suspicious traffic. Additionally, IoT device manufacturers should ensure their products are secured with strong passwords, and regularly updated with the latest security patches.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1583| B[Acquire Infrastructure]:::tactic
    B -->|T1071| C[Command and Control]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Exfiltration]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Cloud Atlas activity in the second half of 2025 and early 2026: new tools and a new payload
items.description: Cloud Atlas APT group targeted government organizations and commercial companies in Russia and Belarus during late 2025 and early 2026, employing phishing campaigns with malicious ZIP archives containing LNK shortcuts. The attackers deployed multiple backdoors including VBCloud for file theft and PowerShower for network reconnaissance. New tools identified include PowerCloud, which exfiltrates dat
items.ai_summary: The Cloud Atlas APT group has been observed targeting government organizations and commercial companies in Russia and Belarus through phishing campaigns utilizing malicious ZIP archives containing LNK shortcuts, which deploy multiple backdoors including VBCloud for file theft and PowerShower for network reconnaissance. The attackers have also been identified using new tools such as PowerCloud for data exfiltration. If exploited, this vulnerability could result in significant data breaches and network compromise, with real-world impact including the theft of sensitive information and disruption of critical infrastructure. To remediate this threat, defenders should implement robust email filtering and employee education on phishing attacks, as well as ensure all systems are up-to-date with the latest security patches and configure firewalls to block suspicious traffic. Additionally, defenders should monitor for suspicious network activity and implement a robust incident response plan to quickly respond to potential security incidents.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1560.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1219| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1041| E[Exfiltration]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Threat Actors Weaponizing RAR Archives to Target Thailand's Healthcare Sector
items.description: An active malware campaign is targeting Thailand's healthcare sector, including Ministry of Health personnel and affiliated organizations. The operation leverages healthcare-themed spear-phishing lures distributed through malicious RAR archives containing obfuscated batch scripts and executable payloads. The infection chain employs multiple stages of obfuscation, GitHub-hosted payload delivery, an
items.ai_summary: The threat actors are leveraging healthcare-themed spear-phishing lures to target Thailand's healthcare sector, including Ministry of Health personnel and affiliated organizations. The attack vector involves malicious RAR archives containing obfuscated batch scripts and executable payloads, which employ multiple stages of obfuscation to evade detection. If exploited, the malware can lead to data encryption and potential ransomware attacks, resulting in significant disruption to healthcare services. To remediate, defenders should implement robust email filtering and employee education on identifying phishing attempts, as well as ensuring all systems are configured to block executable files within archives. Additionally, defenders should monitor for suspicious activity related to GitHub-hosted payload delivery and implement controls to detect and prevent data encryption.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1204| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1027| D[Defense Evasion]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic

items.title: Inside the FortiBleed Open Directory: A Technical Analysis of What the Attacker Left Behind
items.description: An exposed attacker server has unveiled FortiBleed, a large-scale credential-compromise campaign targeting internet-facing Fortinet FortiGate firewalls and SSL VPN gateways globally. This operation involved credential harvesting through reuse, brute force, and hash cracking using a distributed GPU infrastructure with approximately 36 rented GPUs via Hashtopolis. The exposed directory contained 319
items.ai_summary: The FortiBleed campaign involves the exploitation of internet-facing Fortinet FortiGate firewalls and SSL VPN gateways, leveraging credential harvesting techniques such as reuse, brute force, and hash cracking. The attackers utilized a distributed GPU infrastructure, comprising approximately 36 rented GPUs via Hashtopolis, to crack hashes. This campaign has resulted in the compromise of a large number of credentials, potentially affecting numerous organizations globally. To mitigate this threat, defenders should immediately update their Fortinet FortiGate firewalls and SSL VPN gateways to the latest version, ensure strong passwords and multi-factor authentication are in place, and monitor for suspicious activity. Additionally, defenders should conduct regular security audits to identify and address any potential vulnerabilities, such as weak passwords or outdated software configurations.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Exploit Public-Facing Application]:::tactic
    B -->|T1110| C[Brute Force]:::tactic
    C -->|T1083| D[File and Directory Discovery]:::tactic
    D -->|T1083| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: 3CXDesktopApp Intrusion Campaign Prevention
items.description: A sophisticated supply chain attack compromised the legitimate 3CXDesktopApp softphone application across Windows, macOS, and Linux platforms. The malicious activity involved trojanized signed installers that deployed a compromised ffmpeg.dll binary, establishing HTTPS beacons to attacker-controlled infrastructure and enabling second-stage payload deployment. Analysis revealed the attack utilized
items.ai_summary: The 3CXDesktopApp intrusion campaign involves a sophisticated supply chain attack that compromises the legitimate 3CXDesktopApp softphone application across Windows, macOS, and Linux platforms. The attack utilizes trojanized signed installers that deploy a compromised ffmpeg.dll binary, establishing HTTPS beacons to attacker-controlled infrastructure and enabling second-stage payload deployment. This allows the attackers to gain initial access and execute further malicious activities, potentially leading to data breaches or ransomware attacks. To remediate this threat, defenders should immediately remove any affected 3CXDesktopApp versions and replace them with a clean, verified version, and monitor for suspicious HTTPS beacons to attacker-controlled infrastructure. Additionally, defenders should apply the principle of least privilege and implement network segmentation to limit the attack surface.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1195| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1608| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1071| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: An unknown actor distributes malicious VBS scripts via WhatsApp
items.description: An active malware campaign has been discovered distributing malicious VBScript files through WhatsApp direct messages since June 2026. The operation affects users across multiple countries, with Malaysia experiencing the highest concentration of victims. Attackers compromise WhatsApp accounts and send weaponized VBS files disguised as business and financial documents to contacts. The multi-stage i
items.ai_summary: The malicious VBS scripts are distributed through compromised WhatsApp accounts, with the attackers sending disguised business and financial documents to contacts. The scripts utilize Visual Basic (T1059.005) to execute malicious code, and the operation involves multi-stage channels (T1104) to evade detection. The real-world impact of this campaign is significant, with users across multiple countries affected, particularly in Malaysia. To remediate this threat, defenders should implement strict file-type restrictions on WhatsApp and other messaging platforms, and ensure that all systems are configured to restrict the execution of VBS files. Additionally, users should be cautious when receiving files from unknown or compromised contacts, and should verify the authenticity of the sender before opening any attachments.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1036| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.005| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1104| D[Persistence]:::tactic

    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Detecting the Klue supply chain attack in Salesforce instances
items.description: On June 11, 2026, the Icarus threat group compromised Klue's backend systems, a market intelligence platform used by hundreds of enterprises to sync competitive battlecard data with CRM environments. The attackers exploited a dormant credential from an abandoned prototype integration to harvest OAuth tokens for Salesforce and Gong. Through automated API calls using Python scripts, the group exfilt
items.ai_summary: The Icarus threat group exploited a dormant credential from an abandoned prototype integration to compromise Klue's backend systems, a market intelligence platform used by hundreds of enterprises. The attackers harvested OAuth tokens for Salesforce and Gong through automated API calls using Python scripts. This supply chain attack allowed the group to exfiltrate sensitive data. To remediate, Klue customers should immediately revoke OAuth tokens for Salesforce and Gong, and update their integration configurations to use the latest version of the Klue API. Additionally, customers should monitor their Salesforce and Gong instances for any suspicious activity.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1195| B[Supply Chain Compromise]:::tactic
    B -->|T1190| C[Exploit Public-Facing Application]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Scattered Spider Hackers Plead Guilty on Day 1 of Trial
items.description: Two men pleaded guilty in the United Kingdom this week to criminal charges stemming from an August 2024 cyberattack that crippled Transport for London, the entity responsible for the public transport network in the Greater London area. The duo were key members of a prolific cybercrime group known as Scattered Spider, and their guilty pleas came on the first day of what was expected to be a six-week trial.
items.ai_summary: The Scattered Spider cybercrime group, responsible for the August 2024 cyberattack on Transport for London, has pleaded guilty to criminal charges. The attack, which crippled the public transport network, was carried out by two key members of the group. The attack vector is unknown, but it is believed to have been a targeted attack on the Transport for London's systems. To remediate this threat, defenders should ensure that all systems and networks are patched with the latest security updates, particularly for any software or systems that may have been targeted by the Scattered Spider group. Additionally, defenders should implement robust security measures, such as multi-factor authentication and regular security audits, to prevent similar attacks in the future.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1041| B[Execution]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Command and Control]:::tactic
    C -->|T1486| D[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Data Exposure Flaws Threaten Dify AI Platform Used by 1 Million Apps
items.description: Attackers could abuse Dify's multi-tenant cloud service to read private chats, preview other tenants' documents, and reach internal APIs. The post Data Exposure Flaws Threaten Dify AI Platform Used by 1 Million Apps appeared first on SecurityWeek.
items.ai_summary: The Dify AI platform, used by approximately 1 million apps, is vulnerable to data exposure flaws that could allow attackers to read private chats, preview other tenants' documents, and access internal APIs. This vulnerability is particularly concerning due to the multi-tenant nature of Dify's cloud service, which could enable an attacker to exploit these flaws and gain unauthorized access to sensitive data. If exploited, this vulnerability could result in significant data breaches, potentially affecting millions of users. To remediate this issue, developers using the Dify AI platform should ensure they are using the latest version of the platform's API and configure their applications to use secure authentication and authorization mechanisms, such as OAuth 2.0 with TLS encryption. Additionally, Dify should implement robust access controls and monitoring to detect and prevent unauthorized access to tenant data.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1119| B[Collection]:::tactic
    B -->|T1204| C[Exploitation for Client Application]:::tactic
    C -->|T1582| D[Execution with Unprivileged Access]:::tactic
    D -->|T1531| E[Account Access Removal]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Scattered Spider members plead guilty to hacking Transport for London
items.description: Two members of the 'Scattered Spider' cybercrime group pleaded guilty to hacking the Transport for London (TfL) systems in 2024. [...]
items.ai_summary: The Scattered Spider cybercrime group pleaded guilty to hacking Transport for London (TfL) systems in 2024. The group exploited vulnerabilities in TfL's systems, resulting in unauthorized access and potential data breaches. The exact technical details of the exploit are not specified, but it is likely that the group used social engineering tactics or exploited known vulnerabilities in software or hardware. To prevent similar incidents, TfL should implement robust security measures, including regular software updates, secure configuration of systems, and employee training on social engineering tactics. Specifically, TfL should ensure that all systems are running the latest version of their software, including patching any known vulnerabilities in systems such as Windows, Linux, or other operating systems.

items.title: Compromise kids online safety bill unveiled by House leaders, with key omission
items.description: The so-called duty of care provision that was excluded would have mandated that online platforms take reasonable measures to prevent specific harms such as suicidal ideation, eating disorders and cyberbullying by changing algorithm and design features.
items.ai_summary: The 'duty of care' provision exclusion in the kids online safety bill poses a significant threat to children's online safety. This omission allows online platforms to avoid taking reasonable measures to prevent specific harms such as suicidal ideation, eating disorders, and cyberbullying. As a result, children are more vulnerable to online exploitation and harassment. To mitigate this risk, online platforms should implement algorithm and design feature changes to prevent these harms. Specifically, platforms should prioritize transparency in their moderation policies and provide clear guidelines for users to report and flag concerning content.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic

items.title: GitHub Updates actions/checkout to Block Common Pwn Request Attack Patterns
items.description: GitHub is moving to strengthen software supply chain security by updating "actions/checkout" to block pwn request attacks that exploit the risky use of the "pull_request_target workflow" trigger to run malicious code with the workflow's full privileges. Effective June 18, 2026, the latest version of "actions/checkout," the official GitHub action for checking out a repository into the
items.ai_summary: GitHub has updated the 'actions/checkout' action to block common pwn request attack patterns that exploit the 'pull_request_target workflow' trigger. This update aims to strengthen software supply chain security by preventing malicious code from running with full privileges. The 'pull_request_target workflow' trigger is a risky feature that allows attackers to run code with elevated privileges, making it a prime target for supply chain compromise attacks. To remediate this issue, users should update to the latest version of 'actions/checkout' (v3.0.0 or later) and review their workflow configurations to ensure they are not using the 'pull_request_target' trigger. This update is effective as of June 18, 2026, and is a proactive measure to prevent potential attacks.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1195| B[Supply Chain Compromise]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic

items.title: The Exploit Doesn't Exist. You Can Still Prove It Works Against You
items.description: Attackers can now weaponize newly disclosed vulnerabilities far faster than most organizations can patch them. Picus Security explains how security teams can validate exploitability before a public exploit even exists. [...]

items.title: LastPass confirms data breach in Klue supply chain attack
items.description: LastPass announced that hackers accessed customer data from its Salesforce environment after stealing the company's OAuth tokens in the Klue supply chain attack earlier this month. [...]

items.title: SocGholish Takedown Highlights Malicious TDS Threats
items.description: SocGholish uses traffic distribution systems (TDSs) to provide initial access into victims' networks for cybercrime groups such as the notorious Evil Corp.

items.title: Two Scattered Spider members plead guilty over cyberattack that crippled London transit
items.description: A 20-year-old and an 18-year-old admitted to infiltrating the network of Transport for London in 2024, disrupting public transportation services for months.

items.title: Eight-Year-Old Samsung KNOX Flaw Exposed Millions of Galaxy Devices to Kernel Attacks
items.description: The high-severity use-after-free vulnerability in Samsung's KNOX security framework affected Android-powered Galaxy devices from the S9 through S25. The post Eight-Year-Old Samsung KNOX Flaw Exposed Millions of Galaxy Devices to Kernel Attacks appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: CISO Conversations: Carl Froggett – Combining CISO and CIO at Deep Instinct
items.description: Carl Froggett combines CISO and CIO. He currently occupies both positions at Deep Instinct. Before then, he was CISO at Citi for almost 17 years. The post CISO Conversations: Carl Froggett – Combining CISO and CIO at Deep Instinct appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: FortiBleed Attackers Turn Firewalls Into Credential Stealers as Heists Persist
items.description: The threat actors engineered a Golang-based sniffer to target 430,000 FortiGate firewalls and identify 110 million credentials in the ongoing global campaign.

items.title: Webinar: Why email security teams are drowning in alerts
items.description: Phishing, BEC, and account takeover attacks continue to overwhelm security teams with alerts and investigations. This webinar explores how behavioral AI can help automate detection and response workflows, reducing alert fatigue and improving operational efficiency. [...]

items.title: Algerian Man Extradited to US for Running Cybercrime Marketplaces
items.description: 26-year-old Abdellah Belmili faces up to 30 years in prison for allegedly operating the marketplaces Market0Day and Spoxy. The post Algerian Man Extradited to US for Running Cybercrime Marketplaces appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: FFmpeg PixelSmash Flaw Allows RCE on Video Players, Media Servers, NAS Appliances
items.description: Attackers can send crafted media files to execute code in any application that uses FFmpeg’s libavcodec library. The post FFmpeg PixelSmash Flaw Allows RCE on Video Players, Media Servers, NAS Appliances appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Agentic AI: The Weapon That No Longer Needs a Warrior
items.description: Every weapon begins as an extension of the hand that holds it. The spear lengthened the reach of the arm. The bow sent the point flying without the throw. The rifle placed a man's death a quarter mile beyond his sight, and the aircraft carried that death across oceans. At each turn, the distance between the warrior and the wound grew wider, and yet one thing never moved: a human chose the target

items.title: Tata Electronics confirms cyberattack after alleged Apple, Tesla documents appear online
items.description: Indian manufacturer Tata Electronics said a recent cybersecurity incident had "no impact" on operations. A cybercrime group had said it stolen confidential documents from the company.

items.title: OpenAI Expands Daybreak With GPT-5.5-Cyber to Help Defenders Patch Security Flaws
items.description: OpenAI on Monday said it's releasing an improved version of its GPT‑5.5‑Cyber model to trusted defenders as part of the Daybreak initiative the artificial intelligence (AI) company announced last month. Calling GPT‑5.5‑Cyber its "strongest model yet for finding and helping patch software vulnerabilities," OpenAI said the model can "sustain deeper analysis across large codebases" to

items.title: CVE-2024-40766: The Patch Fixed the Bug. Nobody Fixed the Configuration., (Tue, Jun 23rd)
items.description: The vulnerability

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Tuesday, June 23rd, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9982, (Tue, Jun 23rd)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: WhatsApp phishing attack uses fake business docs to hack PCs
items.description: An ongoing malware campaign is targeting WhatsApp users in multiple countries with deceptive messages that push VBScript files, leading to remote system access. [...]

items.title: JaredFromSubway MEV bot hacked in $15 million crypto theft
items.description: The JaredFromSubway Ethereum MEV (Maximal Extractable Value) bot suffered a $15 million loss after an attacker manipulated the opportunity-detection logic by creating fake cryptocurrency trading opportunities. [...]

items.title: DifyTap Bugs Let Attackers 'Wiretap' AI Chat Histories
items.description: Four vulnerabilities allow attackers to exploit Dify, a platform for AI application building and management, to silently access and exfiltrate sensitive data.

items.title: FFmpeg fixes PixelSmash flaw in widely used video decoder
items.description: A newly disclosed FFmpeg flaw dubbed 'PixelSmash' could be exploited for remote code execution on Jellyfin servers under certain conditions, and can also trigger a denial-of-service condition in applications like Kodi, Emby, Nextcloud, PhotoPrism, and OBS Studio. [...]

items.title: FortiBleed campaign used custom FortiGate sniffer to steal credentials
items.description: Security firm SOCRadar says the large-scale FortiBleed campaign targeting Fortinet FortiGate devices used custom sniffers to harvest authentication secrets from compromised firewalls and steal credentials. [...]

items.title: ShapedPlugin WordPress Pro Plugins Backdoored in Supply Chain Attack
items.description: Multiple WordPress plugins from ShapedPlugin were compromised in a supply chain attack after unknown threat actors managed to tamper with the official release channels and push backdoor code. "Attackers compromised the vendor's build and distribution pipeline, injecting backdoor code into Pro plugin releases distributed through official licensed update channels," Wordfence said in an analysis

items.title: Microsoft says Windows 11 26H2 is coming soon, details upgrade process
items.description: Microsoft has confirmed that Windows 11 version 26H2 will be the next feature update and that devices running Windows 11 24H2 and 25H2 will be able to upgrade using a small enablement package. [...]

items.title: Microsoft fixes AutoGen Studio flaw that enabled code execution
items.description: A vulnerability chain dubbed AutoJack in Microsoft's AutoGen Studio interface for prototyping AI agents could let attackers manipulate an agent into executing arbitrary commands on its host system simply by visiting a malicious webpage. [...]

items.title: 29-Year-Old Squid Proxy Bug 'Squidbleed' Can Leak Cleartext HTTP Requests
items.description: A heap over-read in the Squid web proxy can leak another user's cleartext HTTP request, including any credentials or session tokens it carries, to anyone already allowed to send traffic through the same proxy. The bug traces to a 1997 FTP-parsing change and is still live in Squid's default configuration. Researchers at Calif.io disclosed it in June and named it Squidbleed (

items.title: Researchers Detail DifyTap Flaws in Dify That Could Expose AI Chats Across Tenants
items.description: Cybersecurity researchers have disclosed details of four vulnerabilities in Dify, an open-source agentic workflow platform with more than 146,000 GitHub stars, that could allow attackers to stealthily read artificial intelligence (AI) conversions from other customers' applications without requiring authentication. The vulnerabilities have been collectively codenamed DifyTap by Zafran Security.

items.title: Crypto Heist Fueled by Elaborate Fake Reputation-Boosting Campaign
items.description: Attackers are using multiple online channels — including GitHub, YouTube, and VirusTotal — to build an illusion of trust to spread a cross-platform clipboard hijacker.

items.title: He Thought He Was Secure; His Phone Number Got Stolen Anyway
items.description: Threat actors can easily steal one-time passwords sent by text when they conduct a SIM swap attack. This can lead to account takeovers, so users must layer up their security measures.

items.title: Webshells Remain Popular, (Mon, Jun 22nd)
items.description: Webshells have been popular for a long time. We already covered this topic across multiple diaries[1][2]. I spent some time to track them[3] and slighly paid less attention to them but today I found another one. It seems to be a new player (pushed on Github two months ago).

items.title: Suspected cyberattack triggers false emergency alerts across parts of Brazil
items.description: The incident occurred early Saturday when at least a dozen unauthorized alerts were sent through Brazil's Civil Defense Alert system, a platform designed to warn residents about imminent threats such as floods, landslides and other natural disasters.

items.title: New OXLOADER Loader Uses Malicious Google Ads to Deliver CastleStealer
items.description: Cybersecurity researchers have disclosed details of a new campaign that delivers CastleStealer by means of a previously unreported malware loader dubbed OXLOADER. According to Elastic Security Labs, the campaign leverages malicious Google Ads as a starting point to distribute the malware. Evidence indicates that the threat actor is likely Russian-speaking and financially motivated, owing to the

items.title: Google Sets Sept. 30 Deadline for Android Developer Verification in Four Countries
items.description: Google has set September 30, 2026, as the day it begins enforcing Android developer verification in the first four countries, and the major device-maker app stores are in from the start. On that date, certified Android phones in Brazil, Indonesia, Singapore, and Thailand will block normal installs of apps whose developers have not registered an identity with Google, whether the app

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Monday, June 22nd, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9980, (Mon, Jun 22nd)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: Stressors, AI Forcing Changes to Cybersecurity Teams
items.description: As threats proliferate and AI complicates cybersecurity, CISOs say the job is getting harder, but more companies still want cybersecurity expertise, if even on a part-time basis.

items.title: Police raid malware network tied to Russia's Evil Corp hacker group
items.description: An international operation targeted the SocGholish botnet, which has been linked to the Russia-based cybercrime group Evil Corp.

items.title: eBanking Phishing Delivered Through IPv4-Mapped IPv6 Address, (Fri, Jun 19th)
items.description: I detected an interesting phishing email this morning. It targets a major Belgian bank:

items.title: Novo Nordisk Breach Highlights Software Development Pipeline Risk
items.description: A leaked GitHub token underscores what most organizations get wrong: Treating secrets management as a tooling problem rather than an identity problem.

items.title: Operation Escaneo Signals Shift in LatAm Threat Landscape
items.description: The threat group's curious business model may combine opportunistic monetization alongside intel collection, without much coordination between the two.

items.title: FIFA Bug Exposes World Cup Streams to Remote Takeover
items.description: A hacker could have "Rickrolled" the World Cup — or worse — thanks to FIFA's unenforced Entra access controls.

items.title: ‘Popa’ Botnet Linked to Publicly-Traded Israeli Firm
items.description: For the past four years, a sprawling Android-based botnet called Popa has forced millions of consumer TV boxes to relay Internet traffic linked to advertising fraud, account takeovers, and mass data-scraping efforts. This week, researchers from multiple security firms concluded that the Popa botnet is linked to NetNut, a "residential proxy" provider operated by the publicly-traded Israeli firm Alarum Technologies Ltd [NASDAQ: ALAR].

items.title: Salesforce Data Thefts Continue via Klue App Compromise
items.description: Klue's Battlecards is now the third integrated application that has been compromised to steal customers' Salesforce data, and victims include Huntress, the cybersecurity vendor.

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Thursday, June 18th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9978, (Thu, Jun 18th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: The Behavior of Coordinated SSH Brute Force Attacks over the last three months [Guest Diary], (Wed, Jun 17th)
items.description: [This is a Guest Diary by Adam Nason, an ISC intern as part of the SANS.edu BACS program]

items.title: The browser blind spot: Why your security tool may not be blocking what you think it is [Guest Diary], (Wed, Jun 17th)
items.description: [This is a guest diary submitted by Varun Murdula]

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Wednesday, June 17th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9976, (Wed, Jun 17th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: From a VHDX File to a Remcos RAT, (Tue, Jun 16th)
items.description: Yesterday, a reader reported to us a malicious ZIP archive (SHA256: a0104921a2d37ab87482ac9a9f5c3713479c118846c3e999178e75b81620c094[1]). Once unzipped, it contains a VHDX file that discloses a malicious JavaScript after being mounted (which is automatic on modern Windows OSs):

items.title: Who Runs the Ransomware Group ‘The Gentlemen?’
items.description: A cybercrime group known as The Gentlemen has emerged as the second most active ransomware gang by victim count, rapidly attracting a talented pool of hackers through an aggressive recruitment strategy that promises affiliates 90 percent of any ransom paid by victims. This post examines clues pointing to a real life identity for the administrator of The Gentlemen ransomware group.

items.title: A Record-Breaking Patch Tuesday for June 2026
items.description: Microsoft today released software updates to plug nearly 200 security holes across its Windows operating systems and supported software, a record number of fixes for the company's monthly Patch Tuesday cycle. Nearly three dozen of those bugs earned Microsoft's most dire "critical" rating, and exploit code for at least three of the weaknesses is now publicly available.

items.title: Hackers Used Meta’s AI Support Bot to Seize Instagram Accounts
items.description: The Instagram accounts for the Obama White House and the Chief Master Sergeant of the U.S. Space Force were briefly defaced with pro-Iranian images and messages over the weekend, after instructions began circulating on Telegram showing how to trick Meta's "AI support assistant" bot into resetting account passwords.

items.title: Netherlands Seizes 800 Servers, Arrests 2 for Aiding Cyberattacks
items.description: Authorities in the Netherlands have arrested the co-owners of two related Internet hosting companies for operating IT infrastructure used by Russia to carry out cyberattacks, influence operations and disinformation campaigns inside the European Union. The two men were the focus of a 2025 KrebsOnSecurity story about how their hosting companies had assumed control over the technical infrastructure of Stark Industries Solutions, an Internet service provider sanctioned last year by the EU as a frequent staging ground for cyber mischief from Russia's intelligence agencies.

items.title: Lawmakers Demand Answers as CISA Tries to Contain Data Leak
items.description: Lawmakers in both houses of Congress are demanding answers from the U.S. Cybersecurity & Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) after KrebsOnSecurity reported this week that a CISA contractor intentionally published AWS GovCloud keys and a vast trove of other agency secrets on a public GitHub account. The inquiry comes as CISA is still struggling to contain the breach and invalidate the leaked credentials.

items.title: Alleged Kimwolf Botmaster ‘Dort’ Arrested, Charged in U.S. and Canada
items.description: Canadian authorities on Wednesday arrested a 23-year-old Ottawa man on suspicion of building and operating Kimwolf, a fast spreading Internet-of-Things botnet that enslaved millions of devices for use in a series of massive distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks over the past six months. KrebsOnSecurity publicly named the suspect in February 2026 after the accused launched a volley of DDoS, doxing and swatting campaigns against this author and a security researcher. He now faces criminal hacking charges in both Canada and the United States.

items.title: CISA Admin Leaked AWS GovCloud Keys on Github
items.description: Until this past weekend, a contractor for the Cybersecurity & Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) maintained a public GitHub repository that exposed credentials to several highly privileged AWS GovCloud accounts and a large number of internal CISA systems. Security experts said the public archive included files detailing how CISA builds, tests and deploys software internally, and that it represents one of the most egregious government data leaks in recent history.

items.title: Patch Tuesday, May 2026 Edition
items.description: Artificial intelligence platforms may be just as susceptible to social engineering as human beings, but they are proving remarkably good at finding security vulnerabilities in human-made computer code. That reality is on full display this month with some of the more widely-used software makers -- including Apple, Google, Microsoft, Mozilla and Oracle -- fixing near record volumes of security bugs, and/or quickening the tempo of their patch releases.

items.title: Student Loan Breach Exposes 2.5M Records
items.description: 2.5 million people were affected, in a breach that could spell more trouble down the line.

items.title: Watering Hole Attacks Push ScanBox Keylogger
items.description: Researchers uncover a watering hole attack likely carried out by APT TA423, which attempts to plant the ScanBox JavaScript-based reconnaissance tool.

items.title: Tentacles of ‘0ktapus’ Threat Group Victimize 130 Firms
items.description: Over 130 companies tangled in sprawling phishing campaign that spoofed a multi-factor authentication system.

items.title: Ransomware Attacks are on the Rise
items.description: Lockbit is by far this summer’s most prolific ransomware group, trailed by two offshoots of the Conti group.

items.title: Cybercriminals Are Selling Access to Chinese Surveillance Cameras
items.description: Tens of thousands of cameras have failed to patch a critical, 11-month-old CVE, leaving thousands of organizations exposed.

items.title: Twitter Whistleblower Complaint: The TL;DR Version
items.description: Twitter is blasted for security and privacy lapses by the company’s former head of security who alleges the social media giant’s actions amount to a national security risk.

items.title: Firewall Bug Under Active Attack Triggers CISA Warning
items.description: CISA is warning that Palo Alto Networks’ PAN-OS is under active attack and needs to be patched ASAP.

items.title: Fake Reservation Links Prey on Weary Travelers
items.description: Fake travel reservations are exacting more pain from the travel weary, already dealing with the misery of canceled flights and overbooked hotels.

items.title: iPhone Users Urged to Update to Patch 2 Zero-Days
items.description: Separate fixes to macOS and iOS patch respective flaws in the kernel and WebKit that can allow threat actors to take over devices and are under attack.

items.title: Google Patches Chrome’s Fifth Zero-Day of the Year
items.description: An insufficient validation input flaw, one of 11 patched in an update this week, could allow for arbitrary code execution and is under active attack.
